nativeRange.habitat: Found on deeper reef slopes and walls, typically at 15–25 m depth in low-light, slow-flow environments.

diet.notes: Like all reef-building corals, the Duncan Coral relies primarily on photosynthesis through symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living in its tissues, but it responds noticeably to supplemental feeding. Target feeding two to three times per week — delivering food directly to each polyp head individually — supports faster growth and better coloration. Suitable foods include enriched mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, nauplii larvae, cyclops, finely chopped shellfish, and LPS-specific pellets. The coral also absorbs dissolved organics directly from the water column.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS with long sweeper tentacles
large aggressive fish that may nip polyps

summary: The Duncan Coral (Duncanopsammia axifuga) is a branching LPS coral from Australia and the Indo-Pacific, recognized for its large, flowing polyp heads and steady branching growth. It is generally considered one of the more approachable LPS corals once water parameters are stable.
careNotes: The Duncan Coral (Duncanopsammia axifuga) is a fast-growing, branching LPS coral native to Australia and the South China Sea, found naturally at 15–25 m depth in calm, low-light conditions. Each polyp head is roughly 2.5 cm across with long, flowing tentacles that extend fully during feeding and under gentle flow. Colonies branch prolifically with good husbandry and require ample space to avoid crowding neighboring corals. Place toward the bottom of the tank in moderate, indirect flow — the polyps should wave gently without being blown flat, and direct high flow should be avoided. Regular calcium and alkalinity dosing is necessary to support skeletal growth. Care level is generally considered intermediate, though many hobbyists find it one of the more forgiving LPS corals once water parameters are stable. Lifespan in the wild is estimated at decades to over a century; captive specimens have been reliably maintained for multiple decades.
breedingNotes: The Duncan Coral is readily propagated by fragging individual polyp stalks. Each established head produces approximately one new polyp per month under good conditions, and fragments heal quickly and attach to live rock. Sexual reproduction occurs in the wild, though captive sexual breeding has not been commonly reported.

primary.name: World Wide Corals
primary.notes: Primary source page is a generic LPS collection listing; no Duncan-specific data available. Generic LPS guidance (medium-low light, medium flow) is consistent with species-specific sources.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters, lighting, and care level. BRS reports 76–80°F and 150–250 PAR; conservative ranges used to reconcile with other sources.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Detailed care guide; reports 72–78°F, 100–150 PAR, 1.023–1.025 SG, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. Care level listed as beginner.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Confirms family Dendrophylliidae, moderate care level, peaceful temperament, low-to-moderate lighting, bottom-to-middle placement, low flow, kH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025.

additional.name: World Register of Marine Species / Animalia.bio
additional.notes: Taxonomy and native range confirmation: Order Scleractinia, Family Dendrophylliidae; range includes Australia, South China Sea, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Philippines, Vietnam, Taiwan.
